surface.text: Wrote a quick guide on the Central Limit Theorem that my students actually understood. The trick: start with dice, not formulas. Substrate has the full walkthrough with LaTeX, code, and visualizations.

content.description: An intuitive guide to the Central Limit Theorem using dice-rolling simulations

blocks.text: Why Does Everything Look Like a Bell Curve?

blocks.body:
Roll one die: every number from 1-6 is equally likely. Flat distribution.

Roll two dice and sum them: 7 is the most common outcome. The distribution starts to curve.

Roll 30 dice and average them: you get something remarkably close to a perfect bell curve.

This is the Central Limit Theorem in action.

blocks.source:
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

np.random.seed(42)
n_experiments = 10000

for n_dice in [1, 2, 5, 30]:
    averages = [np.mean(np.random.randint(1, 7, n_dice)) for _ in range(n_experiments)]
    plt.hist(averages, bins=50, alpha=0.6, label=f'{n_dice} dice', density=True)

plt.xlabel('Average value')
plt.ylabel('Density')
plt.title('CLT: Averaging more dice → bell curve')
plt.legend()
plt.show()

blocks.title: The Punchline
blocks.body: It doesn't matter what the original distribution looks like — uniform, skewed, bimodal. Average enough independent samples and the result converges to a normal distribution. That's why the bell curve shows up everywhere: heights, test scores, measurement errors. They're all averages of many small, independent effects.

blocks.title: Seeing Theory — Interactive probability visualizations (Brown University)
